name: Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas – Parque natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia
name_original: Puerto de Robledo desde Las Mestas – Parque natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia
description: Cycle the difficult 35.8-mile Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas, gaining 3820 feet through Parque Natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ladrillar are characterized by mountainous terrain and winding roads through the Comarca de Las Hurdes in Extremadura, Spain. The region features varied elevations, dense native forests, and river valleys, providing a challenging environment for cyclists. Roads often lead through traditional villages and offer panoramic views of the rugged landscape. This area is known for its medium to high mountain routes, suitable for extensive road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Ladrillar The most popular road cycling route is Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas – Parque natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia, a 35.8 miles (57.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 7 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gain through the natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Climb to the village of El Gasco — round trip through Las Hurdes, a difficult 34.6 miles (55.7 km) path. This route offers an immersive experience through mountainous landscapes and traditional Hurdan villages. Local road cyclists also love the Casar de Palomero – Nuñomoral loop from Cabezo, a 59.1 miles (95.1 km) trail leading through river valleys and dense forests, often completed in about 4 hours 56 minutes. Road cycling around Ladrillar is defined by mountainous landscapes, dense native forests, and traditional "alquerías" or hamlets. The network offers challenging routes for experienced cyclists, with some moderate options available. The routes in Ladrillar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ladrillar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ladrillar?
A: Ladrillar and the surrounding Comarca de Las Hurdes offer a wide selection of road cycling routes. There are over 35 routes available, catering to various preferences for exploring the mountainous terrain.

Q: What is the typical terrain and difficulty for road cycling in Ladrillar?
A: The region is characterized by mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes, making it ideal for medium to high mountain road cycling. Most routes are classified as difficult, with some moderate options. You'll encounter winding roads through dense native forests and river valleys, providing a challenging yet rewarding experience.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Ladrillar area?
A: Yes, you can find several circular routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133851" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casar de Palomero – Nuñomoral loop from Cabezo&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 95.1 km trail that takes you through river valleys and dense forests. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23742108" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Hurdes, El Portillo – Portillo Viewpoint loop from Las Mestas&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 29.5 km route.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or natural features to look out for while cycling?
A: The region boasts remarkable natural beauty. You can enjoy panoramic views from areas like Peña de Francia, and discover features such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5334215" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Marcos Lagoon&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5579125" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco de la Olla Natural Pool&lt;/a&gt; near Las Mestas. The routes often wind through dense native forests of holly, oak, and pine, offering a rich natural environment.

Q: Can I find challenging climbs for road cycling in Ladrillar?
A: Absolutely. Ladrillar is known for its challenging ascents. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057565825/puerto-de-robledo-from-las-mestas-parque-natural-de-las-batuecas-sierra-de-francia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation gain, with this particular route climbing over 1,100 meters. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31872322" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casar de Palomero – Robledo Pass loop from Cabezo&lt;/a&gt; is another difficult option with over 2,100 meters of ascent.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Ladrillar?
A: The spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Ladrillar, with milder temperatures. Summers can be hot, especially in the valleys, while winters can bring colder weather to the mountainous areas. Checking local weather forecasts before your ride is always recommended.

Q: Are there any traditional villages or cultural sites to explore along the routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in traditional Hurdan culture. You can cycle through traditional 'alquerías' (hamlets) like Riomalo de Arriba, known for its architecture, or Las Mestas, famous for its honey. Nearby, you can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3822217" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Our Lady of the Peña de Francia&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/433119" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of La Alberca&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is Ladrillar suitable for family-friendly road cycling?
A: The road cycling routes around Ladrillar are predominantly rated as moderate to difficult, featuring significant climbs and descents. While there are no routes specifically classified as easy, experienced families comfortable with mountainous terrain might find some of the shorter moderate routes manageable. Always check the route profile for elevation and distance before setting out.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Ladrillar?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars. Reviewers often praise the stunning mountainous landscapes, the immersive experience through traditional Hurdan villages, and the challenging yet rewarding nature of the routes. Many appreciate the quiet roads and the opportunity to explore the region's unique natural beauty.

Q: Where can I find parking for my car when starting a road cycling route in Ladrillar?
A: While specific cycling-dedicated parking areas might be limited, you can typically find parking in or near the main villages and hamlets within Ladrillar and the wider Las Hurdes region, such as Las Mestas or Cabezo, which serve as common starting points for many routes. Always check local signage for parking regulations.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, as you cycle through the traditional 'alquerías' and villages of Las Hurdes, you'll find opportunities to stop for refreshments. Hamlets like Las Mestas are known for their local products, including honey, and often have small establishments where cyclists can take a break and refuel.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while cycling in the region?
A: The dense native forests and well-preserved ecosystems around Ladrillar support diverse wildlife. You might spot wild boars, mountain goats, foxes, deer, and various large birds of prey. The natural environment is a significant part of the cycling experience here.
tips: Las Mestas is a charming village in the Las Hurdes region at the foot of the Sierra de Francia. You must stop at the natural pools of Charco de la Olla to take a bath or cool off on your cycling route or see the small Church of Nuestra Señora del Carmen built with typical stone from the area.
Vegas de Coria is a small town on the banks of the Hurdano River. Cool off in the water from the river, a fluvial beach created by a small reservoir.
Nice town in the heart of Las Hurdes

Iconic cycling climb with many switchbacks providing incredible views. The ascent begins from the town of Nuñomoral to the Port of Robledo in the Sierra de Francia in the region of Las Hurdes. It's a long climb to 1100 masl, so keep up.
In the town of Serradilla del Llano, on the CV-149 highway, we will head towards the province of Cáceres. The port begins at the bridge over the Agaddon River.

faqs.question: Is the road cycling route to Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is considered difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. The ascent of Puerto del Robledo from Las Mestas is a Category 1 climb, stretching 21.5 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of 740 meters and sections reaching a maximum gradient of 9%.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Puerto de Robledo climb?
faqs.answer: The terrain is mostly paved, offering a challenging road cycling experience. While the general area's road cycling routes are described as having mostly paved surfaces, be prepared for a demanding climb with varying gradients.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the stunning Parque Natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia, known for its deep canyons, diverse vegetation, and local wildlife. You'll pass by the Charco de la Olla Natural Pool in Las Mestas, the Hurdano River in Vegas de Coria, and the settlement of Nuñomoral. The climb itself leads to the Robledo Pass, offering scenic views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific seasonal recommendations aren't detailed, considering the significant elevation and natural park setting, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling in this region, avoiding the extreme heat of summer or potential winter conditions at higher altitudes.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route in Las Mestas?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas in Las Mestas are not detailed, as a starting point for a popular cycling route, you can typically find roadside parking or designated areas within the village. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or is there an entrance fee for cycling in Parque Natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia. However, always respect park regulations, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of the natural environment.

faqs.question: Is the Puerto de Robledo from Las Mestas route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling route. While dogs are generally allowed in natural parks, they should be kept on a leash, especially given the presence of wildlife. However, the focus of this route is road cycling, which may not be ideal for dogs.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The full komoot tour, which includes the Puerto de Robledo climb, is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 7 minutes to complete for an average cyclist. This duration can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other recognized cycling paths. Approximately 26% of the route covers part of the [CIMA CC08] El Robledo (Casares) path, and a small portion (1%) intersects with the [CIMA SA07] El Portillo de las Batuecas.

faqs.question: What makes the Puerto de Robledo climb particularly notable for cyclists?
faqs.answer: The Puerto de Robledo climb is notable for its challenging profile as a Category 1 ascent and its inclusion in Stage 16 of the Vuelta a España in 2020. This solidifies its reputation as a significant and rewarding climb for road cyclists seeking both physical exertion and breathtaking scenery within the natural park.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restaurants near the start of the route in Las Mestas?
faqs.answer: Las Mestas is a small village, and while specific cafes or restaurants are not detailed, it's common for such starting points to have basic amenities. It's always a good idea to check local listings or bring your own provisions, especially for a longer ride.
